+# Redis Cluster v2 Proposal
+
+## Table of Contents
+
+<!-- toc -->
+- [Summary](#summary)
+- [Motivation](#motivation)
+- [Design](#design)
+    - [Terminology](#terminology)
+    - [High Level Overview](#high-level-overview)
+    - [The Topology Director](#the-topology-director)
+      - [Interfaces](#interfaces)
+    - [The Failover Coordinator](#the-failover-coordinator)
+        - [Interfaces](#interfaces)
+    - [The Node](#the-node)
+        - [Interfaces](#interfaces)
+- [Flows](#flows)
+    - [Membership and role management](#membership-and-role-management)
+        - [Cluster Creation](#cluster-creation)
+        - [Join](#join)
+        - [Remove Node](#remove-node)
+        - [Migrate A Node between Failover Coordinators](#migrate-a-node-between-failover-coordinators)
+    - [Failover](#failover)
+        - [Failover Coordinator Tick](#failover-coordinator-tick)
+    - [Resharding](#resharding)
+- [Flotilla Implementation Details](#flotilla-implementation-details)
+    - [Roles](#roles)
+    - [Node and Failover Coordinator IDs](#node-and-failover-coordinator-ids)
+    - [Publish/Subscribe](#publish/subscribe) 
+    - [Reference Implementation](#reference-implementation)
+        - [Road to Production](#road-to-production) 
+        - [Potential Enhancements](#potential-enhancements)
+<!-- /toc -->
+
+## Summary
+
+A new approach to Redis Clustering is proposed in order to:
+
+- Provide predictable behavior & correctness guarantees across the various possible failure modes
+- Create a minimal, well-defined internal interface to the clustering code so alternative approaches can easily replace the
+  entire clustering functionality at compile time.
+- Support a significantly higher number of shards
+- Significantly reduce failover time to under 10 seconds (at worse) and under 5 seconds in some configurations 
+- Allow for alternative consistency mode of operation
+- Long term: Support a clustered, but un-sharded deployment mode with no cross-slot limitations, which will allow us to deprecate all Sentinel code
+- Long term: Enable centralized management for global configuration settings & Redis Functions
+
+This new approach is:
+- Based on a two-tier strongly consistent, consensus-based control plane
+- Maintains full backwards compatibility with data path Cluster commands (MOVED, NODES, SLOTS, ...), but not 
+  administrative commands (SETSLOT, FORGET, MEET,...)
+- Has a consistent source of truth for all cluster metadata
+- Requires the shards to reconcile their state according to the above source
+- Maintains the ability to scale down to a bare minimum 3 redis-server processes
+- Is called Flotilla :)
+
+And is intended to fully replace the current clustering implementation after a deprecation period in which the two 
+implementations will live side-by-side with compile time flag to chose between the two.
+
+## Motivation
+
+The current Redis Cluster is based on Gossip with the use of epochs to attempt to 
+control/reduce the impact of concurrent changes. It is easy to set up but has a lot of issues when performing day 2
+operations & scaling.
+
+For example:
+- Reliance on primaries (that may fail-over and move around) for quorum makes it impossible to guarantee availability using rack awareness.
+- Node removal has an inherent race condition which can lead to nodes being re-added
+- No single source of truth about who the primary is for a given slot range
+
+Our main goal with this new approach is to provide a more robust solution, with an easy-to-understand
+behavior and predictable failure modes. Beyond this, we also aim to substantially improve the scalability in order to
+be able to easily support a cluster with thousands of shards.
+
+## Design
+
+### Terminology
+
+| Term | Description |
+| --- | --- |
+| Node | A Single redis-server process |
+| Shard | A logical entity comprised of a group of nodes owning the same slots. It is automatically allocated when a new unique slot range is assigned to a node and automatically deallocated when all slots are moved away |
+| Host | A physical / virtual machine hosting the Flotilla processes |
+
+### High Level Overview
+
+Flotilla is composed of two tiers of strongly consistent, consensus-based control plane systems - The Topology Director 
+(TD) and the Failover Coordinator (FC).
+
+The Topology Director is the source of truth for shard existence and metadata, slot range allocation and Failover Coordinator
+assignments. In addition to the above, it also propagates failover information across the various Failover Coordinators.
+
+The Failover Coordinators is the source of truth for Primary/Replica roles. Each Failover Coordinators is assigned a subset 
+of the total nodes in the cluster, receives & propagates heartbeat information from them and enables them to make failover decisions in a
+consistent & timely manner.
+
+Maintaining these two tiers allows scaling the number of nodes while:
+- Maintaining an upper bound over the time required to decide and propagate failover decisions.
+While the time required to propagate the updated primary/replica status depends on the number of Failover Coordinators in the deployment
+the maximum number of these should always be small enough to allow us to guarantee a substantially better SLA then what
+the current implementation provides.
+- Limiting the clustering overhead on individual nodes in the deployment.
+Depending on pub/sub mode, an individual node only needs to maintain a connection to its Failover Coordinator (and primary/replicas).
+
+While Flotilla can be implemented using any sort of strongly consistent system, the reference implementation we propose
+uses Redis to host the Topology Director & Failover Coordinator logic. This allows for different deployment modes depending on 
+the needs and orchestrating abilities of the administrator:
+- Full Blown: Dedicated nodes holding the Topology Directors and multiple Failover Coordinators dividing the data nodes among them.
+- Midrange: Dedicated nodes holding both the Topology Director and Failover Coordinator (with just one Failover Coordinator owning all the data nodes).
+- Lightweight: Topology Director and Failover Coordinator located on a subset of data nodes. This removes the need to maintain and orchestrate
+non-data nodes at the cost of potentially impacting performance on the dual-use nodes.
+- Naive: Topology Director and Failover Coordinator located on all nodes. This allows for the quickest bootstrapping, but severely limits scalability.
+
+![Flotilla High Level overview](images/high_level.jpg?raw=true "High Level Overview")
+
+### The Topology Director
+
+The Topology Director serves as the source of truth for the cluster topology (excluding primary/replica status) and helps propagate
+information from Failover Coordinators. It never handles any user data, only topology metadata.
+Since it owns the metadata of the entire cluster it can allow changing this metadata while preserving the strong
+consistency guarantees we desire.
+For example - it can ensure that if a Shard holds a slot marked as Migrating, there's a corresponding Shard with the slot marked as Importing.
+We also rely on the Failover Coordinator to distribute the primary/replica state of individual nodes - This state is received periodically
+from every Failover Coordinator, cached in the Topology Director and relayed to all other Failover Coordinators when they send their state.
+
+- A completely reactive entity
+- Receives requests to alter the cluster topology and adjusts the internal state as needed
+- Validates change requests for correctness
+- Responds to topology queries by the other components
+
+#### Interfaces
+
+The Topology Director interface is fully declarative other than join/remove flows. This allows for easy orchestration via various tools.
+
+With the administrator, directly for creating the cluster / updating and removing nodes:
+
+**FLOTILLA.TOPOLOGY**: Similar to CLUSTER.SLOTS/NODES - response lists all the Flotilla nodes and their metadata  
+**FLOTILLA.REMOVE_NODE**: Remove a given node from the cluster  
+**FLOTILLA.ASSIGN_SLOTS**: Assign a set slots to specified Shard  
+**FLOTILLA.UPDATE_NODES**: Change the topology of a list of nodes    
+
+    Update/Assign_slots commands can specify the epoch they are based on in order to allow flotilla to reject outdated requests.
+    Update allows:
+    - Taking ownership of unassigned slots
+    - Moving an unallocated node / shard between Failover Coordinators
+  
+With the administrator via the individual node for adding nodes / Failover Coordinators to Flotilla:
+
+**FLOTILLA.CREATE_TD**: Create the Flotilla Cluster from the current node  
+**FLOTILLA.CREATE_FC**: Create a new Failover Coordinator entity from the current node  
+**FLOTILLA.JOIN_TD**: Add the node to be part of the Topology Director quorum  
+**FLOTILLA.JOIN_FC**: Add the node to be part of a given Failover Coordinator  
+**FLOTILLA.JOIN_NODE**: Add the data node to Flotilla  
+    
+    All Join/Creation requests can specify a desired Shard ID/slot range up front or set it later using an UPDATE command.
+    Responses contain the new node/Failover Coordinator IDs and the topology information needed for the new node to start functioning - a list
+    of all Topology Director nodes and all the nodes belongining to the relevant Failover Coordinator.

I want to make a counter suggestion that nodes should not have a concept of where FCs/TDs are. (not sure if here is the best spot, but there is an implied assumption that nodes should know where FCs and TDs are which I want to challenge). There are two designs here, either nodes establish connections to FCs to send health data OR FCs reach out to nodes to get health information. Note, even in the second option, we can still push health information from the data nodes.

Why is this useful?
1. FCs are part of the control plane, and will have more reliable and consistent access to which nodes they own. Once they learn what nodes they are supposed to connect to, they can go do that. 
2. The data cluster code becomes simpler, since it just opens a port and has to respond to commands. It doesn't need discovery logic, it doesn't need to know any IPs of the control plane, and more importantly doesn't care about what is going on in the CP.
3. You can more easily protect the CP from a network perspective. This might be a lot, but often times you might want a network barrier to split the data plane and control plane, which can allow connections to be established from DP->CP and not vice versa.
4. Can prevent thundering herd issues where all the data nodes try to reach out to the FC at the same time. This is mitigated by exponential backoff as well, but it's harder to rate limit if a node is misbehaving and keeps overwhelming the FC.
5. If a node is lost, it doesn't need to reach out to the TD to learn about the topology, since the FC will eventually reach back out.
6. The implementation is significantly more flexible to be changed, since the API boundary is smaller between the data nodes and the CP. Making breaking changes here will be easier.
7. We don't need to persist stuff as completely to handle restarts. Realistically just the "cluster identifier" and "our identifier" so that we can trust that the thing reaching out to us is something we trust and it can verify we are who we say we are. Everything else we throw away and rebuild on restart. This partially mediates zombies.

Why is this not so great?
1. If a node dies, the FC will continue trying to reach out to it, and it won't be able to reach it. It will need to know what to about that. (It will at least know that it's "unreachable" though, which is useful)
2. Slightly harder discoverability, since the node itself will not have logs about why it can't be reached. You'll need to find the FC to figure out what is wrong. Note sure this is really all that different.
3. We probably still want individual data nodes to reach out to the TD for joining. I believe this is useful for K8s deployments.
5. Doesn't support nodes "moving". I don't know if this is important, but maybe it is? We at AWS assume nodes locations are fixed, but that might be untrue in some types of configurations. It seems like removing + re-adding is always net safer.

Just different:
1. Nodes would need a port to listen on, we have the clusterbus today but maybe we don't want to use that since the protocol is annoying. I guess that is TBd.

+# Redis Cluster v2 Proposal
+
+## Table of Contents
+
+<!-- toc -->
+- [Summary](#summary)
+- [Motivation](#motivation)
+- [Design](#design)
+    - [Terminology](#terminology)
+    - [High Level Overview](#high-level-overview)
+    - [The Topology Director](#the-topology-director)
+      - [Interfaces](#interfaces)

+        - [Interfaces](#interfaces)
+    - [The Node](#the-node)
+        - [Interfaces](#interfaces)
+- [Flows](#flows)
+    - [Membership and role management](#membership-and-role-management)
+        - [Cluster Creation](#cluster-creation)
+        - [Join](#join)
+        - [Remove Node](#remove-node)
+        - [Migrate A Node between Failover Coordinators](#migrate-a-node-between-failover-coordinators)
+    - [Failover](#failover)
+        - [Failover Coordinator Tick](#failover-coordinator-tick)
+    - [Resharding](#resharding)
+- [Flotilla Implementation Details](#flotilla-implementation-details)
+    - [Roles](#roles)
+    - [Node and Failover Coordinator IDs](#node-and-failover-coordinator-ids)
+    - [Publish/Subscribe](#publish/subscribe) 
+    - [Reference Implementation](#reference-implementation)
+        - [Road to Production](#road-to-production) 
+        - [Potential Enhancements](#potential-enhancements)
+<!-- /toc -->
+
+## Summary
+
+A new approach to Redis Clustering is proposed in order to:
+
+- Provide predictable behavior & correctness guarantees across the various possible failure modes
+- Create a minimal, well-defined internal interface to the clustering code so alternative approaches can easily replace the
+  entire clustering functionality at compile time.
+- Support a significantly higher number of shards
+- Significantly reduce failover time to under 10 seconds (at worse) and under 5 seconds in some configurations 
+- Allow for alternative consistency mode of operation
+- Long term: Support a clustered, but un-sharded deployment mode with no cross-slot limitations, which will allow us to deprecate all Sentinel code
+- Long term: Enable centralized management for global configuration settings & Redis Functions
+
+This new approach is:
+- Based on a two-tier strongly consistent, consensus-based control plane
+- Maintains full backwards compatibility with data path Cluster commands (MOVED, NODES, SLOTS, ...), but not 
+  administrative commands (SETSLOT, FORGET, MEET,...)
+- Has a consistent source of truth for all cluster metadata
+- Requires the shards to reconcile their state according to the above source
+- Maintains the ability to scale down to a bare minimum 3 redis-server processes
+- Is called Flotilla :)
+
+And is intended to fully replace the current clustering implementation after a deprecation period in which the two 
+implementations will live side-by-side with compile time flag to chose between the two.
+
+## Motivation
+
+The current Redis Cluster is based on Gossip with the use of epochs to attempt to 
+control/reduce the impact of concurrent changes. It is easy to set up but has a lot of issues when performing day 2
+operations & scaling.
+
+For example:
+- Reliance on primaries (that may fail-over and move around) for quorum makes it impossible to guarantee availability using rack awareness.
+- Node removal has an inherent race condition which can lead to nodes being re-added
+- No single source of truth about who the primary is for a given slot range
+
+Our main goal with this new approach is to provide a more robust solution, with an easy-to-understand
+behavior and predictable failure modes. Beyond this, we also aim to substantially improve the scalability in order to
+be able to easily support a cluster with thousands of shards.
+
+## Design
+
+### Terminology
+
+| Term | Description |
+| --- | --- |
+| Node | A Single redis-server process |
+| Shard | A logical entity comprised of a group of nodes owning the same slots. It is automatically allocated when a new unique slot range is assigned to a node and automatically deallocated when all slots are moved away |
+| Host | A physical / virtual machine hosting the Flotilla processes |
+
+### High Level Overview
+
+Flotilla is composed of two tiers of strongly consistent, consensus-based control plane systems - The Topology Director 
+(TD) and the Failover Coordinator (FC).
+
+The Topology Director is the source of truth for shard existence and metadata, slot range allocation and Failover Coordinator
+assignments. In addition to the above, it also propagates failover information across the various Failover Coordinators.
+
+The Failover Coordinators is the source of truth for Primary/Replica roles. Each Failover Coordinators is assigned a subset 
+of the total nodes in the cluster, receives & propagates heartbeat information from them and enables them to make failover decisions in a
+consistent & timely manner.
+
+Maintaining these two tiers allows scaling the number of nodes while:
+- Maintaining an upper bound over the time required to decide and propagate failover decisions.
+While the time required to propagate the updated primary/replica status depends on the number of Failover Coordinators in the deployment
+the maximum number of these should always be small enough to allow us to guarantee a substantially better SLA then what
+the current implementation provides.
+- Limiting the clustering overhead on individual nodes in the deployment.
+Depending on pub/sub mode, an individual node only needs to maintain a connection to its Failover Coordinator (and primary/replicas).
+
+While Flotilla can be implemented using any sort of strongly consistent system, the reference implementation we propose
+uses Redis to host the Topology Director & Failover Coordinator logic. This allows for different deployment modes depending on 
+the needs and orchestrating abilities of the administrator:
+- Full Blown: Dedicated nodes holding the Topology Directors and multiple Failover Coordinators dividing the data nodes among them.
+- Midrange: Dedicated nodes holding both the Topology Director and Failover Coordinator (with just one Failover Coordinator owning all the data nodes).
+- Lightweight: Topology Director and Failover Coordinator located on a subset of data nodes. This removes the need to maintain and orchestrate
+non-data nodes at the cost of potentially impacting performance on the dual-use nodes.
+- Naive: Topology Director and Failover Coordinator located on all nodes. This allows for the quickest bootstrapping, but severely limits scalability.
+
+![Flotilla High Level overview](images/high_level.jpg?raw=true "High Level Overview")
+
+### The Topology Director
+
+The Topology Director serves as the source of truth for the cluster topology (excluding primary/replica status) and helps propagate
+information from Failover Coordinators. It never handles any user data, only topology metadata.
+Since it owns the metadata of the entire cluster it can allow changing this metadata while preserving the strong
+consistency guarantees we desire.
+For example - it can ensure that if a Shard holds a slot marked as Migrating, there's a corresponding Shard with the slot marked as Importing.
+We also rely on the Failover Coordinator to distribute the primary/replica state of individual nodes - This state is received periodically
+from every Failover Coordinator, cached in the Topology Director and relayed to all other Failover Coordinators when they send their state.
+
+- A completely reactive entity
+- Receives requests to alter the cluster topology and adjusts the internal state as needed
+- Validates change requests for correctness
+- Responds to topology queries by the other components
+
+#### Interfaces
+
+The Topology Director interface is fully declarative other than join/remove flows. This allows for easy orchestration via various tools.
+
+With the administrator, directly for creating the cluster / updating and removing nodes:
+
+**FLOTILLA.TOPOLOGY**: Similar to CLUSTER.SLOTS/NODES - response lists all the Flotilla nodes and their metadata  
+**FLOTILLA.REMOVE_NODE**: Remove a given node from the cluster  
+**FLOTILLA.ASSIGN_SLOTS**: Assign a set slots to specified Shard  
+**FLOTILLA.UPDATE_NODES**: Change the topology of a list of nodes    
+
+    Update/Assign_slots commands can specify the epoch they are based on in order to allow flotilla to reject outdated requests.
+    Update allows:
+    - Taking ownership of unassigned slots
+    - Moving an unallocated node / shard between Failover Coordinators
+  
+With the administrator via the individual node for adding nodes / Failover Coordinators to Flotilla:
+
+**FLOTILLA.CREATE_TD**: Create the Flotilla Cluster from the current node  
+**FLOTILLA.CREATE_FC**: Create a new Failover Coordinator entity from the current node  
+**FLOTILLA.JOIN_TD**: Add the node to be part of the Topology Director quorum  
+**FLOTILLA.JOIN_FC**: Add the node to be part of a given Failover Coordinator  
+**FLOTILLA.JOIN_NODE**: Add the data node to Flotilla  
+    
+    All Join/Creation requests can specify a desired Shard ID/slot range up front or set it later using an UPDATE command.
+    Responses contain the new node/Failover Coordinator IDs and the topology information needed for the new node to start functioning - a list
+    of all Topology Director nodes and all the nodes belongining to the relevant Failover Coordinator.
discussion_comments.comment_body:
I agree we need to think about it more. Intuitively I find it easier to think in terms of pull when a a large fleet of N worker entities talks to a single control plane element.

Some arguments:
* As @madolson mentioned, we need that mode anyway for registration.
* A node that can't talk/doesn't hear from the CP has more information about why that's the case (i.e. connection error, auth error, etc. vs just sitting there waiting for the call).

discussion_comments.comment_body:
I personally like the pull model as well since it provides a very clean separation between data plane and control plane and helps reduce config conflicts. 

>If a node dies, the FC will continue trying to reach out to it, and it won't be able to reach it. It will need to know what to about that. (It will at least know that it's "unreachable" though, which is useful)

This isn't that different than FC not receiving a data node's health ping IMO.

>Slightly harder discoverability, since the node itself will not have logs about why it can't be reached. You'll need to find the FC to figure out what is wrong. Note sure this is really all that different.

Seems the same to me :-). I would imagine admins talk to TD to find out cluster states anyway.

>We probably still want individual data nodes to reach out to the TD for joining. I believe this is useful for K8s deployments.

If we go with "add" as opposed to "join", I could imagine an admin asking TD to "add" an FC or data node to the cluster. With "join", the request would have to go to the node to be added itself and this indeed leads to a "push" model for registration. Another very different idea though would be  a 100% declarative model where we/admins don't explicitly join nodes to the cluster. Instead, we/admins create VMs/containers and then pass their network identities to TD along with the high level cluster states, such as N shards with M replicas, etc.

>A node that can't talk/doesn't hear from the CP has more information about why that's the case (i.e. connection error, auth error, etc. vs just sitting there waiting for the call).

Not sure if the "why" is important. I think the failure detection on the node side can still be achieved by stipulating a certain number of PINGs within a given internal from an FC.

discussion_comments.comment_body:
Took my time to think about this one.
To make sure we're aligned terminology wise  -- Push == Node initiates connections to FC, FC to TD (like current spec).

I'd argue that the only true advantage of Pull here is the ability to isolate the control plane network from the data plane. All other Pros have an equivalent Con. Considering the original points raised:
1. As mentioned, discovery becomes significantly more complex. If we want to prevent IP changes from breaking the cluster we now need to maintain internal DNS resolution for all data nodes and not just for the FC/TD (eg - thousands of records instead of <10 in most cases). 
2.  Moving this logic to the FC layer makes it more complicated and increases the impact it will have in the naive deployment cases where it runs side-by-side on a data node (and also means that alternative implementations become more difficult). It also means that a data node must wait for the FC to connect to it in order to initiate failover - instead of immediately triggering one when it notes it is unable to connect to the primary/receives the failover command.
3. Agree about the network isolation aspect, though it is worth mentioning this is only relevant for folks running with completely separate data/FC/TD nodes AND  (probably only the bigger Redis-as-a-Service vendors).
4. A misbehaving FC will generate load on plane components, implementing this logic on the FC side is more difficult then simple jitter+backoff and might make it harder to predict failover times, means that implementing a TD/FC becomes more complicated.
5. FCs will keep retrying dead/unreachable nodes forever.
6. I'm not sure why switching to Pull makes the API simpler. It makes some of the implementation easier / harder and moves some of the more networking oriented bits into the FC, but unless you're proposing a bigger change it does not remove the need for the interface itself.
7. Is that really significant? As you wrote we still need to persist the identity + some cluster ID/secret so there's still the need for storage.

In addition to the above (and the other cons listed in the thread):
- Joining nodes become a two step operation for 'simple' admins (Will have to go to the data node to configure the cluster secret, and then send the request to the TD to add the new node).
- Without significant other changes, Heartbeats/Failovers will become a two/four-step operation (instead of two/three): FC asks for HB, Receives a response with an out-of-date epoch, sends the relevant topology, receives the updated HB.

+        - [Join](#join)
+        - [Remove Node](#remove-node)
+        - [Migrate A Node between Failover Coordinators](#migrate-a-node-between-failover-coordinators)
+    - [Failover](#failover)

+    - [Resharding](#resharding)
+- [Flotilla Implementation Details](#flotilla-implementation-details)
+    - [Roles](#roles)
+    - [Node and Failover Coordinator IDs](#node-and-failover-coordinator-ids)
+    - [Publish/Subscribe](#publish/subscribe) 
+    - [Reference Implementation](#reference-implementation)
+        - [Road to Production](#road-to-production) 
+        - [Potential Enhancements](#potential-enhancements)
+<!-- /toc -->
+
+## Summary
+
+A new approach to Redis Clustering is proposed in order to:
+
+- Provide predictable behavior & correctness guarantees across the various possible failure modes
+- Create a minimal, well-defined internal interface to the clustering code so alternative approaches can easily replace the
+  entire clustering functionality at compile time.
+- Support a significantly higher number of shards
+- Significantly reduce failover time to under 10 seconds (at worse) and under 5 seconds in some configurations 
+- Allow for alternative consistency mode of operation
+- Long term: Support a clustered, but un-sharded deployment mode with no cross-slot limitations, which will allow us to deprecate all Sentinel code
+- Long term: Enable centralized management for global configuration settings & Redis Functions
+
+This new approach is:
+- Based on a two-tier strongly consistent, consensus-based control plane
+- Maintains full backwards compatibility with data path Cluster commands (MOVED, NODES, SLOTS, ...), but not 
+  administrative commands (SETSLOT, FORGET, MEET,...)
+- Has a consistent source of truth for all cluster metadata
+- Requires the shards to reconcile their state according to the above source
+- Maintains the ability to scale down to a bare minimum 3 redis-server processes
+- Is called Flotilla :)
+
+And is intended to fully replace the current clustering implementation after a deprecation period in which the two 
+implementations will live side-by-side with compile time flag to chose between the two.
+
+## Motivation
+
+The current Redis Cluster is based on Gossip with the use of epochs to attempt to 
+control/reduce the impact of concurrent changes. It is easy to set up but has a lot of issues when performing day 2
+operations & scaling.
+
+For example:
+- Reliance on primaries (that may fail-over and move around) for quorum makes it impossible to guarantee availability using rack awareness.
+- Node removal has an inherent race condition which can lead to nodes being re-added
+- No single source of truth about who the primary is for a given slot range
+
+Our main goal with this new approach is to provide a more robust solution, with an easy-to-understand
+behavior and predictable failure modes. Beyond this, we also aim to substantially improve the scalability in order to
+be able to easily support a cluster with thousands of shards.
+
+## Design
+
+### Terminology
+
+| Term | Description |
+| --- | --- |
+| Node | A Single redis-server process |
+| Shard | A logical entity comprised of a group of nodes owning the same slots. It is automatically allocated when a new unique slot range is assigned to a node and automatically deallocated when all slots are moved away |
+| Host | A physical / virtual machine hosting the Flotilla processes |
+
+### High Level Overview
+
+Flotilla is composed of two tiers of strongly consistent, consensus-based control plane systems - The Topology Director 
+(TD) and the Failover Coordinator (FC).
+
+The Topology Director is the source of truth for shard existence and metadata, slot range allocation and Failover Coordinator
+assignments. In addition to the above, it also propagates failover information across the various Failover Coordinators.
+
+The Failover Coordinators is the source of truth for Primary/Replica roles. Each Failover Coordinators is assigned a subset 
+of the total nodes in the cluster, receives & propagates heartbeat information from them and enables them to make failover decisions in a
+consistent & timely manner.
+
+Maintaining these two tiers allows scaling the number of nodes while:
+- Maintaining an upper bound over the time required to decide and propagate failover decisions.
+While the time required to propagate the updated primary/replica status depends on the number of Failover Coordinators in the deployment
+the maximum number of these should always be small enough to allow us to guarantee a substantially better SLA then what
+the current implementation provides.
+- Limiting the clustering overhead on individual nodes in the deployment.
+Depending on pub/sub mode, an individual node only needs to maintain a connection to its Failover Coordinator (and primary/replicas).
+
+While Flotilla can be implemented using any sort of strongly consistent system, the reference implementation we propose
+uses Redis to host the Topology Director & Failover Coordinator logic. This allows for different deployment modes depending on 
+the needs and orchestrating abilities of the administrator:
+- Full Blown: Dedicated nodes holding the Topology Directors and multiple Failover Coordinators dividing the data nodes among them.
+- Midrange: Dedicated nodes holding both the Topology Director and Failover Coordinator (with just one Failover Coordinator owning all the data nodes).
+- Lightweight: Topology Director and Failover Coordinator located on a subset of data nodes. This removes the need to maintain and orchestrate
+non-data nodes at the cost of potentially impacting performance on the dual-use nodes.
+- Naive: Topology Director and Failover Coordinator located on all nodes. This allows for the quickest bootstrapping, but severely limits scalability.
+
+![Flotilla High Level overview](images/high_level.jpg?raw=true "High Level Overview")
+
+### The Topology Director
+
+The Topology Director serves as the source of truth for the cluster topology (excluding primary/replica status) and helps propagate
+information from Failover Coordinators. It never handles any user data, only topology metadata.
+Since it owns the metadata of the entire cluster it can allow changing this metadata while preserving the strong
+consistency guarantees we desire.
+For example - it can ensure that if a Shard holds a slot marked as Migrating, there's a corresponding Shard with the slot marked as Importing.
+We also rely on the Failover Coordinator to distribute the primary/replica state of individual nodes - This state is received periodically
+from every Failover Coordinator, cached in the Topology Director and relayed to all other Failover Coordinators when they send their state.
+
+- A completely reactive entity
+- Receives requests to alter the cluster topology and adjusts the internal state as needed
+- Validates change requests for correctness
+- Responds to topology queries by the other components
+
+#### Interfaces
+
+The Topology Director interface is fully declarative other than join/remove flows. This allows for easy orchestration via various tools.
+
+With the administrator, directly for creating the cluster / updating and removing nodes:
+
+**FLOTILLA.TOPOLOGY**: Similar to CLUSTER.SLOTS/NODES - response lists all the Flotilla nodes and their metadata  
+**FLOTILLA.REMOVE_NODE**: Remove a given node from the cluster  
+**FLOTILLA.ASSIGN_SLOTS**: Assign a set slots to specified Shard  
+**FLOTILLA.UPDATE_NODES**: Change the topology of a list of nodes    
+
+    Update/Assign_slots commands can specify the epoch they are based on in order to allow flotilla to reject outdated requests.
+    Update allows:
+    - Taking ownership of unassigned slots
+    - Moving an unallocated node / shard between Failover Coordinators
+  
+With the administrator via the individual node for adding nodes / Failover Coordinators to Flotilla:
+
+**FLOTILLA.CREATE_TD**: Create the Flotilla Cluster from the current node  
+**FLOTILLA.CREATE_FC**: Create a new Failover Coordinator entity from the current node  
+**FLOTILLA.JOIN_TD**: Add the node to be part of the Topology Director quorum  
+**FLOTILLA.JOIN_FC**: Add the node to be part of a given Failover Coordinator  
+**FLOTILLA.JOIN_NODE**: Add the data node to Flotilla  
+    
+    All Join/Creation requests can specify a desired Shard ID/slot range up front or set it later using an UPDATE command.
+    Responses contain the new node/Failover Coordinator IDs and the topology information needed for the new node to start functioning - a list
+    of all Topology Director nodes and all the nodes belongining to the relevant Failover Coordinator.

discussion_comments.comment_body:
> As mentioned, discovery becomes significantly more complex. If we want to prevent IP changes from breaking the cluster we now need to maintain internal DNS resolution for all data nodes and not just for the FC/TD (eg - thousands of records instead of <10 in most cases).

This might be a fair point highlighting my cloud bias :) The folks I've talked to from other places say that this isn't a big deal, as in most cases nodes are still typically either assigned a permanent IP or hostname within most production networks. 

> FCs will keep retrying dead/unreachable nodes forever.

This gets at whether or not a cluster is an interchangeable bag of nodes, or nodes that represent a specific topology. The trend seems to the latter, so I assume retrying forever is the correct behavior, until it's reconfigured.

> Is that really significant? As you wrote we still need to persist the identity + some cluster ID/secret so there's still the need for storage.

These items are persistent and don't really change over time (presumably), which makes it easier to persist them. They could be provided on an ephemeral disk or startup arguments. So, yes, it is somewhat significant. 

> I'm not sure why switching to Pull makes the API simpler. It makes some of the implementation easier / harder and moves some of the more networking oriented bits into the FC, but unless you're proposing a bigger change it does not remove the need for the interface itself.

The specific point is that I see it as very useful if data nodes only know about the FC interface, they don't know about the TD. I see this as useful because I view the TD as ultimately an optimization for specialized configurations, as a lot of other folks have addressed, and don't want to mandate it as part of the interface. I don't see how this doesn't dramatically simplify contract and flows. I can see plenty of implementations, including a very simplified version of the one being proposed, which don't have the specific forced architecture of a separate TD and FC. 

Another sub point which I think was missed, is that the FC can reach out to the node, but the node can still follow up by pushing heart beats back at any time. These should be long-lived established connections, so there is no need for the establisher of the connection to necessarily drive all of the communication.

+    - [Reference Implementation](#reference-implementation)
+        - [Road to Production](#road-to-production) 
+        - [Potential Enhancements](#potential-enhancements)
+<!-- /toc -->
+
+## Summary
+
+A new approach to Redis Clustering is proposed in order to:
+
+- Provide predictable behavior & correctness guarantees across the various possible failure modes
+- Create a minimal, well-defined internal interface to the clustering code so alternative approaches can easily replace the
+  entire clustering functionality at compile time.
+- Support a significantly higher number of shards
+- Significantly reduce failover time to under 10 seconds (at worse) and under 5 seconds in some configurations 
+- Allow for alternative consistency mode of operation
+- Long term: Support a clustered, but un-sharded deployment mode with no cross-slot limitations, which will allow us to deprecate all Sentinel code
+- Long term: Enable centralized management for global configuration settings & Redis Functions
+
+This new approach is:
+- Based on a two-tier strongly consistent, consensus-based control plane
+- Maintains full backwards compatibility with data path Cluster commands (MOVED, NODES, SLOTS, ...), but not 
+  administrative commands (SETSLOT, FORGET, MEET,...)
+- Has a consistent source of truth for all cluster metadata
+- Requires the shards to reconcile their state according to the above source
+- Maintains the ability to scale down to a bare minimum 3 redis-server processes
+- Is called Flotilla :)
+
+And is intended to fully replace the current clustering implementation after a deprecation period in which the two 
+implementations will live side-by-side with compile time flag to chose between the two.
+
+## Motivation
+
+The current Redis Cluster is based on Gossip with the use of epochs to attempt to 
+control/reduce the impact of concurrent changes. It is easy to set up but has a lot of issues when performing day 2
+operations & scaling.
+
+For example:
+- Reliance on primaries (that may fail-over and move around) for quorum makes it impossible to guarantee availability using rack awareness.
+- Node removal has an inherent race condition which can lead to nodes being re-added
+- No single source of truth about who the primary is for a given slot range
+
+Our main goal with this new approach is to provide a more robust solution, with an easy-to-understand
+behavior and predictable failure modes. Beyond this, we also aim to substantially improve the scalability in order to
+be able to easily support a cluster with thousands of shards.
+
+## Design
+
+### Terminology
+
+| Term | Description |
+| --- | --- |
+| Node | A Single redis-server process |
+| Shard | A logical entity comprised of a group of nodes owning the same slots. It is automatically allocated when a new unique slot range is assigned to a node and automatically deallocated when all slots are moved away |
+| Host | A physical / virtual machine hosting the Flotilla processes |
+
+### High Level Overview
+
+Flotilla is composed of two tiers of strongly consistent, consensus-based control plane systems - The Topology Director 
+(TD) and the Failover Coordinator (FC).
+
+The Topology Director is the source of truth for shard existence and metadata, slot range allocation and Failover Coordinator
+assignments. In addition to the above, it also propagates failover information across the various Failover Coordinators.
+
+The Failover Coordinators is the source of truth for Primary/Replica roles. Each Failover Coordinators is assigned a subset 
+of the total nodes in the cluster, receives & propagates heartbeat information from them and enables them to make failover decisions in a
+consistent & timely manner.
+
+Maintaining these two tiers allows scaling the number of nodes while:
+- Maintaining an upper bound over the time required to decide and propagate failover decisions.
+While the time required to propagate the updated primary/replica status depends on the number of Failover Coordinators in the deployment
+the maximum number of these should always be small enough to allow us to guarantee a substantially better SLA then what
+the current implementation provides.
+- Limiting the clustering overhead on individual nodes in the deployment.
+Depending on pub/sub mode, an individual node only needs to maintain a connection to its Failover Coordinator (and primary/replicas).
+
+While Flotilla can be implemented using any sort of strongly consistent system, the reference implementation we propose
+uses Redis to host the Topology Director & Failover Coordinator logic. This allows for different deployment modes depending on 
+the needs and orchestrating abilities of the administrator:
+- Full Blown: Dedicated nodes holding the Topology Directors and multiple Failover Coordinators dividing the data nodes among them.
+- Midrange: Dedicated nodes holding both the Topology Director and Failover Coordinator (with just one Failover Coordinator owning all the data nodes).
+- Lightweight: Topology Director and Failover Coordinator located on a subset of data nodes. This removes the need to maintain and orchestrate
+non-data nodes at the cost of potentially impacting performance on the dual-use nodes.
+- Naive: Topology Director and Failover Coordinator located on all nodes. This allows for the quickest bootstrapping, but severely limits scalability.
+
+![Flotilla High Level overview](images/high_level.jpg?raw=true "High Level Overview")
+
+### The Topology Director
+
+The Topology Director serves as the source of truth for the cluster topology (excluding primary/replica status) and helps propagate
+information from Failover Coordinators. It never handles any user data, only topology metadata.
+Since it owns the metadata of the entire cluster it can allow changing this metadata while preserving the strong
+consistency guarantees we desire.
+For example - it can ensure that if a Shard holds a slot marked as Migrating, there's a corresponding Shard with the slot marked as Importing.
+We also rely on the Failover Coordinator to distribute the primary/replica state of individual nodes - This state is received periodically
+from every Failover Coordinator, cached in the Topology Director and relayed to all other Failover Coordinators when they send their state.
+
+- A completely reactive entity
+- Receives requests to alter the cluster topology and adjusts the internal state as needed
+- Validates change requests for correctness
+- Responds to topology queries by the other components
+
+#### Interfaces
+
+The Topology Director interface is fully declarative other than join/remove flows. This allows for easy orchestration via various tools.
+
+With the administrator, directly for creating the cluster / updating and removing nodes:
+
+**FLOTILLA.TOPOLOGY**: Similar to CLUSTER.SLOTS/NODES - response lists all the Flotilla nodes and their metadata  
+**FLOTILLA.REMOVE_NODE**: Remove a given node from the cluster  
+**FLOTILLA.ASSIGN_SLOTS**: Assign a set slots to specified Shard  
+**FLOTILLA.UPDATE_NODES**: Change the topology of a list of nodes    
+
+    Update/Assign_slots commands can specify the epoch they are based on in order to allow flotilla to reject outdated requests.
+    Update allows:
+    - Taking ownership of unassigned slots
+    - Moving an unallocated node / shard between Failover Coordinators
+  
+With the administrator via the individual node for adding nodes / Failover Coordinators to Flotilla:
+
+**FLOTILLA.CREATE_TD**: Create the Flotilla Cluster from the current node  
+**FLOTILLA.CREATE_FC**: Create a new Failover Coordinator entity from the current node  
+**FLOTILLA.JOIN_TD**: Add the node to be part of the Topology Director quorum  
+**FLOTILLA.JOIN_FC**: Add the node to be part of a given Failover Coordinator  
+**FLOTILLA.JOIN_NODE**: Add the data node to Flotilla  
+    
+    All Join/Creation requests can specify a desired Shard ID/slot range up front or set it later using an UPDATE command.
+    Responses contain the new node/Failover Coordinator IDs and the topology information needed for the new node to start functioning - a list
+    of all Topology Director nodes and all the nodes belongining to the relevant Failover Coordinator.

@madolson I'm afraid I still don't understand how switching to a Pull based approach makes the API simpler. Unless you're thinking about a much bigger change then just Push->Pull then beyond JOIN vs ADD the change is just in who opens the relevant connection, not in how the flow works

If the main issue is around the Node->TD connection for the initial JOIN request we can always support proxing that through the relevant FC... The node doesn't really care where the upstream request is really handled.

>This might be a fair point highlighting my cloud bias :) The folks I've talked to from other places say that this isn't a big deal, as in most cases nodes are still typically either assigned a permanent IP or hostname within most production networks.

This is often (but not always) true when you're talking VMs / physical instances, but is very much false when in a containerized environment where the IPs change whenever a pod is rescheduled.

> Another sub point which I think was missed, is that the FC can reach out to the node, but the node can still follow up by pushing heart beats back at any time. These should be long-lived established connections, so there is no need for the establisher of the connection to necessarily drive all of the communication.

This is similar to long polling... it introduces more complexity on the FC side.

> I'm afraid I still don't understand how switching to a Pull based approach makes the API simpler. Unless you're thinking about a much bigger change then just Push->Pull then beyond JOIN vs ADD the change is just in who opens the relevant connection, not in how the flow works

I didn't understand this statement. The change is not just about who opens the relevant connection, but about who is responsible for maintaining the connection. Typically the control plane is responsible for establishing health check connections and sending messages, not the data plane. In the current model, the data path is responsible for knowing where the control plane is and sending messages to it. I would expect a model where the data path knows nothing, and receives updates from the control plane about state.

Both models can reasonably work. 

>If the main issue is around the Node->TD connection for the initial JOIN request we can always support proxing that through the relevant FC... The node doesn't really care where the upstream request is really handled.

I don't know enough about containerized environments to make a claim about this. I've been told this is trivial to solve with hostnames, but I myself am not an active user of kubernetes or some similar system to know if that is true.

> This is similar to long polling... it introduces more complexity on the FC side.

I'm not convinced this is materially much more complex.

+- Midrange: Dedicated nodes holding both the Topology Director and Failover Coordinator (with just one Failover Coordinator owning all the data nodes).
+- Lightweight: Topology Director and Failover Coordinator located on a subset of data nodes. This removes the need to maintain and orchestrate
+non-data nodes at the cost of potentially impacting performance on the dual-use nodes.

+consistency guarantees we desire.

discussion_comments.comment_body:
I have a change of heart. I now view FCs/TDs metadata nodes of the cluster hence part of the data plane as opposed to the control plane. The difference between them matters to me because the clean separation of CP/DP is no longer a concern with this view. This view is also consistent with how we have been regularly discussing cluster v1 and v2 side by side and considering them different solutions for the same clustering problem.  After mulling over this some more, I now feel that the push model is easier to work with and yields more reliability. I see 3 scenarios being affected by the pull-vs-push decision: steady state with node failures, cluster bootstrapping, and scaling-out. The latter two involve the discovery problem that @ushachar called out.

1. steady state with node failures
I now think the push model is more reliable than the pull model as it involves less work on the FC side (the repetitive polling of down nodes). The thundering herd risk associated with the push model can be mitigated by a rate limiter on the FC side but the repetitive polling would add more stress on the FC node especially in the large scale event case and when a large cluster is involved. Given that FCs (and TDs) own the cluster metadata, their health is of higher priority IMO.
2. cluster bootstrapping and scaling-out
I agree discovery is at the core in these two scenarios. If my understanding is correct, the data node will be created/bootstrapped "out-of-band", meaning not through the new cluster V2 APIs. With the "pull" model, as has been pointed out, the identities of the new data nodes need to be noted down somewhere after their creation. We then need to partition the new data nodes and assign them to their corresponding FCs. This can also be achieved by passing the identities of the new data nodes to the TDs and then having the TDs split up the data nodes somehow. With the "push" model, FCs and TDs can be created first. Data nodes can then be created/bootstrapped with their owning FC's identity. In theory, it is also possible to bootstrap data nodes with just TDs' network identities and again have TDs redirect the new data nodes to the proper FCs. There seems to be less states to manage and again less work on the FCs/TDs side.

+<!-- /toc -->
+
+## Summary
+
+A new approach to Redis Clustering is proposed in order to:
+
+- Provide predictable behavior & correctness guarantees across the various possible failure modes
+- Create a minimal, well-defined internal interface to the clustering code so alternative approaches can easily replace the
+  entire clustering functionality at compile time.
+- Support a significantly higher number of shards
+- Significantly reduce failover time to under 10 seconds (at worse) and under 5 seconds in some configurations 
+- Allow for alternative consistency mode of operation
+- Long term: Support a clustered, but un-sharded deployment mode with no cross-slot limitations, which will allow us to deprecate all Sentinel code
+- Long term: Enable centralized management for global configuration settings & Redis Functions
+
+This new approach is:
+- Based on a two-tier strongly consistent, consensus-based control plane
+- Maintains full backwards compatibility with data path Cluster commands (MOVED, NODES, SLOTS, ...), but not 
+  administrative commands (SETSLOT, FORGET, MEET,...)
+- Has a consistent source of truth for all cluster metadata
+- Requires the shards to reconcile their state according to the above source
+- Maintains the ability to scale down to a bare minimum 3 redis-server processes
+- Is called Flotilla :)
+
+And is intended to fully replace the current clustering implementation after a deprecation period in which the two 
+implementations will live side-by-side with compile time flag to chose between the two.
+
+## Motivation
+
+The current Redis Cluster is based on Gossip with the use of epochs to attempt to 
+control/reduce the impact of concurrent changes. It is easy to set up but has a lot of issues when performing day 2
+operations & scaling.
+
+For example:
+- Reliance on primaries (that may fail-over and move around) for quorum makes it impossible to guarantee availability using rack awareness.
+- Node removal has an inherent race condition which can lead to nodes being re-added
+- No single source of truth about who the primary is for a given slot range
+
+Our main goal with this new approach is to provide a more robust solution, with an easy-to-understand
+behavior and predictable failure modes. Beyond this, we also aim to substantially improve the scalability in order to
+be able to easily support a cluster with thousands of shards.
+
+## Design
+
+### Terminology
+
+| Term | Description |
+| --- | --- |
+| Node | A Single redis-server process |
+| Shard | A logical entity comprised of a group of nodes owning the same slots. It is automatically allocated when a new unique slot range is assigned to a node and automatically deallocated when all slots are moved away |
+| Host | A physical / virtual machine hosting the Flotilla processes |

+
+Flotilla is composed of two tiers of strongly consistent, consensus-based control plane systems - The Topology Director 
+(TD) and the Failover Coordinator (FC).
+
+The Topology Director is the source of truth for shard existence and metadata, slot range allocation and Failover Coordinator
+assignments. In addition to the above, it also propagates failover information across the various Failover Coordinators.
+
+The Failover Coordinators is the source of truth for Primary/Replica roles. Each Failover Coordinators is assigned a subset 
+of the total nodes in the cluster, receives & propagates heartbeat information from them and enables them to make failover decisions in a
+consistent & timely manner.
+
+Maintaining these two tiers allows scaling the number of nodes while:
+- Maintaining an upper bound over the time required to decide and propagate failover decisions.

+the maximum number of these should always be small enough to allow us to guarantee a substantially better SLA then what
+the current implementation provides.
+- Limiting the clustering overhead on individual nodes in the deployment.

+uses Redis to host the Topology Director & Failover Coordinator logic. This allows for different deployment modes depending on

+| Node | A Single redis-server process |
+| Shard | A logical entity comprised of a group of nodes owning the same slots. It is automatically allocated when a new unique slot range is assigned to a node and automatically deallocated when all slots are moved away |
+| Host | A physical / virtual machine hosting the Flotilla processes |
+
+### High Level Overview
+
+Flotilla is composed of two tiers of strongly consistent, consensus-based control plane systems - The Topology Director 
+(TD) and the Failover Coordinator (FC).
+
+The Topology Director is the source of truth for shard existence and metadata, slot range allocation and Failover Coordinator
+assignments. In addition to the above, it also propagates failover information across the various Failover Coordinators.
+
+The Failover Coordinators is the source of truth for Primary/Replica roles. Each Failover Coordinators is assigned a subset 
+of the total nodes in the cluster, receives & propagates heartbeat information from them and enables them to make failover decisions in a
+consistent & timely manner.
+
+Maintaining these two tiers allows scaling the number of nodes while:
+- Maintaining an upper bound over the time required to decide and propagate failover decisions.
+While the time required to propagate the updated primary/replica status depends on the number of Failover Coordinators in the deployment
+the maximum number of these should always be small enough to allow us to guarantee a substantially better SLA then what
+the current implementation provides.
+- Limiting the clustering overhead on individual nodes in the deployment.
+Depending on pub/sub mode, an individual node only needs to maintain a connection to its Failover Coordinator (and primary/replicas).
+
+While Flotilla can be implemented using any sort of strongly consistent system, the reference implementation we propose
+uses Redis to host the Topology Director & Failover Coordinator logic. This allows for different deployment modes depending on 
+the needs and orchestrating abilities of the administrator:
+- Full Blown: Dedicated nodes holding the Topology Directors and multiple Failover Coordinators dividing the data nodes among them.
+- Midrange: Dedicated nodes holding both the Topology Director and Failover Coordinator (with just one Failover Coordinator owning all the data nodes).
+- Lightweight: Topology Director and Failover Coordinator located on a subset of data nodes. This removes the need to maintain and orchestrate
+non-data nodes at the cost of potentially impacting performance on the dual-use nodes.
+- Naive: Topology Director and Failover Coordinator located on all nodes. This allows for the quickest bootstrapping, but severely limits scalability.
+
+![Flotilla High Level overview](images/high_level.jpg?raw=true "High Level Overview")
+
+### The Topology Director
+
+The Topology Director serves as the source of truth for the cluster topology (excluding primary/replica status) and helps propagate
+information from Failover Coordinators. It never handles any user data, only topology metadata.
+Since it owns the metadata of the entire cluster it can allow changing this metadata while preserving the strong
+consistency guarantees we desire.
+For example - it can ensure that if a Shard holds a slot marked as Migrating, there's a corresponding Shard with the slot marked as Importing.
+We also rely on the Failover Coordinator to distribute the primary/replica state of individual nodes - This state is received periodically
+from every Failover Coordinator, cached in the Topology Director and relayed to all other Failover Coordinators when they send their state.
+
+- A completely reactive entity
+- Receives requests to alter the cluster topology and adjusts the internal state as needed
+- Validates change requests for correctness
+- Responds to topology queries by the other components
+
+#### Interfaces
+
+The Topology Director interface is fully declarative other than join/remove flows. This allows for easy orchestration via various tools.
+
+With the administrator, directly for creating the cluster / updating and removing nodes:
+
+**FLOTILLA.TOPOLOGY**: Similar to CLUSTER.SLOTS/NODES - response lists all the Flotilla nodes and their metadata  
+**FLOTILLA.REMOVE_NODE**: Remove a given node from the cluster  
+**FLOTILLA.ASSIGN_SLOTS**: Assign a set slots to specified Shard  
+**FLOTILLA.UPDATE_NODES**: Change the topology of a list of nodes    
+
+    Update/Assign_slots commands can specify the epoch they are based on in order to allow flotilla to reject outdated requests.
+    Update allows:
+    - Taking ownership of unassigned slots
+    - Moving an unallocated node / shard between Failover Coordinators
+  
+With the administrator via the individual node for adding nodes / Failover Coordinators to Flotilla:
+
+**FLOTILLA.CREATE_TD**: Create the Flotilla Cluster from the current node  
+**FLOTILLA.CREATE_FC**: Create a new Failover Coordinator entity from the current node  
+**FLOTILLA.JOIN_TD**: Add the node to be part of the Topology Director quorum  
+**FLOTILLA.JOIN_FC**: Add the node to be part of a given Failover Coordinator  
+**FLOTILLA.JOIN_NODE**: Add the data node to Flotilla  
+    
+    All Join/Creation requests can specify a desired Shard ID/slot range up front or set it later using an UPDATE command.
+    Responses contain the new node/Failover Coordinator IDs and the topology information needed for the new node to start functioning - a list
+    of all Topology Director nodes and all the nodes belongining to the relevant Failover Coordinator.  
+    Full topology will be retrieved later (for most nodes from their Failover Coordinator) in order to minimize load on the Topology Director in very large 
+    clusters.
+  
+With the Failover Coordinators for topology & primary/replica state propagation:
+
+**FLOTILLA.FAILOVER_STATUS**: Sent periodically from every Failover Coordinator
+
+    Includes the last known cluster epoch.  
+    Lists all nodes owned by the Failover Coordinator:
+    - The last configuration epoch they acknowledged   
+    - Their primary/replica status
+    - Potentially (but not needed for Flotilla operations) - metadata like:
+        - Replication offset
+        - Last heard timestamp
+     
+    The response includes:
+    - The last failover status received from all other Failover Coordinators
+    - If epoch is outdated, the current topology
+
+
+### The Failover Coordinator
+
+The Failover Coordinator allows individual nodes to achieve consensus regarding Primary / Replica roles within the shard
+they belong to and also propagates topology state changes to all the nodes it is responsible for.
+
+It periodically sends a snapshot of the primary/replica status of all the nodes it owns to the Topology Director, and
+receives topology updates as the response.
+
+#### Interfaces
+
+The Failover Coordinator does not expose an external interface, it's entirely internal to Flotilla and beyond troubleshooting there's no
+need for the administrator to interact directly with it.
+
+- With the Topology Director for topology & primary/replica state propagation
+- With the nodes for heartbeat, leadership change requests & topology propagation.
+
+### The Node
+
+Each node is responsible to periodically send heartbeat information to the Failover Coordinator which owns it. The response it receives
+contains the updated topology & last heartbeat times of the other nodes in the same shard (the group of nodes
+owning the same slots). The node is responsible for promoting itself by writing the updated status to the Failover Coordinator if it
+detects that its current primary has not sent a heartbeat for a long enough time.
+

+## Summary
+
+A new approach to Redis Clustering is proposed in order to:
+
+- Provide predictable behavior & correctness guarantees across the various possible failure modes
+- Create a minimal, well-defined internal interface to the clustering code so alternative approaches can easily replace the
+  entire clustering functionality at compile time.
+- Support a significantly higher number of shards
+- Significantly reduce failover time to under 10 seconds (at worse) and under 5 seconds in some configurations 
+- Allow for alternative consistency mode of operation
+- Long term: Support a clustered, but un-sharded deployment mode with no cross-slot limitations, which will allow us to deprecate all Sentinel code
+- Long term: Enable centralized management for global configuration settings & Redis Functions
+
+This new approach is:
+- Based on a two-tier strongly consistent, consensus-based control plane
+- Maintains full backwards compatibility with data path Cluster commands (MOVED, NODES, SLOTS, ...), but not 
+  administrative commands (SETSLOT, FORGET, MEET,...)
+- Has a consistent source of truth for all cluster metadata
+- Requires the shards to reconcile their state according to the above source
+- Maintains the ability to scale down to a bare minimum 3 redis-server processes
+- Is called Flotilla :)
+
+And is intended to fully replace the current clustering implementation after a deprecation period in which the two 
+implementations will live side-by-side with compile time flag to chose between the two.
+
+## Motivation
+
+The current Redis Cluster is based on Gossip with the use of epochs to attempt to 
+control/reduce the impact of concurrent changes. It is easy to set up but has a lot of issues when performing day 2
+operations & scaling.
+
+For example:
+- Reliance on primaries (that may fail-over and move around) for quorum makes it impossible to guarantee availability using rack awareness.
+- Node removal has an inherent race condition which can lead to nodes being re-added
+- No single source of truth about who the primary is for a given slot range
+
+Our main goal with this new approach is to provide a more robust solution, with an easy-to-understand
+behavior and predictable failure modes. Beyond this, we also aim to substantially improve the scalability in order to
+be able to easily support a cluster with thousands of shards.
+

+

+

+The Topology Director is the source of truth for shard existence and metadata, slot range allocation and Failover Coordinator
+assignments. In addition to the above, it also propagates failover information across the various Failover Coordinators.

+implementations will live side-by-side with compile time flag to chose between the two.
+
+## Motivation
+
+The current Redis Cluster is based on Gossip with the use of epochs to attempt to 
+control/reduce the impact of concurrent changes. It is easy to set up but has a lot of issues when performing day 2
+operations & scaling.
+
+For example:
+- Reliance on primaries (that may fail-over and move around) for quorum makes it impossible to guarantee availability using rack awareness.
+- Node removal has an inherent race condition which can lead to nodes being re-added
+- No single source of truth about who the primary is for a given slot range
+
+Our main goal with this new approach is to provide a more robust solution, with an easy-to-understand
+behavior and predictable failure modes. Beyond this, we also aim to substantially improve the scalability in order to
+be able to easily support a cluster with thousands of shards.
+
+## Design
+
+### Terminology
+
+| Term | Description |
+| --- | --- |
+| Node | A Single redis-server process |
+| Shard | A logical entity comprised of a group of nodes owning the same slots. It is automatically allocated when a new unique slot range is assigned to a node and automatically deallocated when all slots are moved away |
+| Host | A physical / virtual machine hosting the Flotilla processes |
+
+### High Level Overview
+
+Flotilla is composed of two tiers of strongly consistent, consensus-based control plane systems - The Topology Director 
+(TD) and the Failover Coordinator (FC).
+
+The Topology Director is the source of truth for shard existence and metadata, slot range allocation and Failover Coordinator
+assignments. In addition to the above, it also propagates failover information across the various Failover Coordinators.
discussion_comments.comment_body: If we never propagate primary/replica changes clients which get their topology from FC X would always get a MOVED on their initial connection to nodes in any other FC.
